title: Basbousa
subtitle: Egyptian semolina cake soaked in rose water syrup
excerpt: Basbousa is a dense, moist Egyptian semolina cake baked in a tray and soaked immediately in cold sugar syrup while still hot. The contrast of hot cake and cold syrup is what drives the syrup deep into the crumb. It is cut into diamonds or squares in the tin and eaten at room temperature. The crumb is slightly grainy from the semolina and the flavour is plain and sweet, lifted by the rose water.
sourceNotes: Basbousa is made across Egypt, the Levant, and the wider Middle East under various names; harissa (not the chilli paste), revani in Turkey and Greece, namoura in Lebanon. The semolina-and-syrup combination appears in historical Arab cookery texts from the medieval period. The Egyptian style typically includes yoghurt and coconut; the Levantine version often uses tahini to grease the tin.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 tray (20 × 30 cm), cuts into about 24 pieces
recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before soaking. Soak the thawed pieces in warm syrup for 5 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps at room temperature for 4 days. Improves on day two once the syrup has fully settled.

glossaryTerms.definition: A technique used with soaked cakes — most commonly basbousa, revani, and similar — where cold syrup is poured over hot baked cake immediately on leaving the oven. The temperature difference creates a pressure gradient that pulls the syrup deep into the crumb: the hot cake expands and contracts as it cools, drawing liquid inward. Pouring warm syrup over warm cake produces a shallower soak because the pressure difference is smaller.

content.text: The batter is quick to put together; everything mixed in one bowl, spread into a greased tin, scored before baking, and a whole blanched almond pressed into each diamond if you like. The syrup goes on cold, straight from the fridge, the moment the cake comes out of the oven. This is the
that makes basbousa what it is. Pour it all on at once; it will seem like too much.

Make the syrup first so it has time to cool completely. Combine 200 g sugar and 200 ml water in a small saucepan over medium heat. Stir to dissolve, bring to a simmer, and cook for 3 minutes. Remove from the heat, add 1 tbsp rose water and the juice of half a lemon. Leave to cool to room temperature, then refrigerate until needed.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C fan. Grease a 20 × 30 cm baking tray. Combine 250 g fine semolina, 120 g caster sugar, 40 g desiccated coconut, and 1 tsp baking powder in a bowl. Add 200 g yoghurt, 80 g melted butter, and 1 tsp vanilla extract. Stir until evenly combined; the batter will be thick. Spread into the prepared tin. With a sharp knife, score the surface into 24 diamond shapes. Press a blanched almond into each diamond if using. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until deep golden and a skewer comes out clean.

Remove the cake from the oven. Immediately pour the cold syrup evenly over the hot cake; all of it. The cake will hiss and absorb the liquid. Leave in the tin for at least 30 minutes. Re-cut along the score lines before serving. Serve at room temperature.

items.problem: Cake is dry and the syrup sat on the surface
items.solution: The syrup was not cold enough or the cake was not hot enough. The syrup must be cold from the fridge; the cake must go straight from the oven. Make sure all the syrup is poured on while the cake is still steaming.

items.problem: Cake is soggy and falling apart
items.solution: The syrup quantity is intentionally large, and the cake needs time to absorb it properly. Allow the full 30 minutes in the tin before cutting. The texture firms on standing.

items.problem: Cake did not rise and is very dense
items.solution: The batter may have been overworked after adding the baking powder, or the baking powder was past its use-by date. Stir just until combined and use fresh baking powder.
